questions.q: The episode gives B-X-I-R as the master list of user-action vulnerabilities across both parts. What does B-X-I-R stand for?
options: Broken authentication and session management, Cross-site attacks, Invalid forwarding and redirecting, Race conditions
Buffer overflow, Cross-origin sharing, Input validation, Replay attacks
Broken access, XML injection, Insecure storage, Resource exhaustion
Backups, eXploits, Incident response, Recovery
questions.explanation: B-X-I-R is the umbrella hook for the whole user-action vulnerability list: Broken authentication and session management, Cross-site attacks, Invalid forwarding and redirecting, and Race conditions. Part 1 covers the X and the I (cross-site attacks and invalid redirects); Part 2 takes the B and the R. Option D mixes in the survival lifecycle (harden, breach handling, recovery) from a different part of the recap, not the vulnerability list.

questions.q: Identify the three types of cross-site scripting named in the episode.
options: Inline, external, and embedded XSS
Reflected, stored (persistent), and DOM-based XSS
Client-side, server-side, and database XSS
Active, passive, and chained XSS
questions.explanation: The episode names exactly three types: reflected XSS (script arrives on a request and is immediately echoed back), stored or persistent XSS (script is saved on the server and served to everyone who views the page), and DOM-based XSS (the injection happens entirely in client-side JavaScript). Option C is tempting because XSS involves client and server, but those are not the named categories.

questions.q: Explain the single root cause of cross-site scripting and the primary fix, as stated in the episode.
options: The root cause is weak passwords; the fix is multi-factor authentication
The root cause is an expired TLS certificate; the fix is renewing HTTPS
The root cause is a missing firewall rule; the fix is closing unused ports
The root cause is untrusted input placed into a page without encoding so the browser runs it as script; the fix is output encoding (escaping) the input on the way into the page
questions.explanation: XSS occurs when untrusted input is echoed into a page without encoding, so the browser interprets it as executable script instead of text. The primary fix is output encoding (escaping), which converts dangerous characters to harmless HTML entities so a less-than sign displays as text rather than starting a tag. This is the same disease as SQL injection — input becoming code — just in the browser instead of the database.

questions.q: Why is stored (persistent) XSS described as the worst of the three XSS types?
options: It is the only type that can steal a session cookie
The script is saved on the server and served to every viewer automatically, with no clicking required
It is the only type that works without an internet connection
It permanently corrupts the server's database files
questions.explanation: Stored XSS saves the script on the server — in a comment, profile, or forum post — and then serves it to everyone who views that page, hitting every viewer automatically with no clicking required. Reflected XSS, by contrast, requires the attacker to trick a victim into clicking a crafted link. Option A is wrong because any XSS type can steal a session cookie; it is the automatic, every-viewer reach that makes stored XSS the worst.

questions.q: Outline how cross-site request forgery (CSRF) works against a logged-in bank user.
options: The attacker steals the user's password by injecting a keylogging script into the bank's login page
The attacker guesses the user's session token by brute force and replays it
The attacker tricks the logged-in user's browser into sending an unintended request to the bank, and the browser automatically attaches the existing session cookie so the request looks authentic
The attacker intercepts the connection and decrypts the session cookie in transit
questions.explanation: CSRF tricks a logged-in user's browser into sending a request the user never intended to a site where they are already authenticated. A hidden form or image on a malicious page fires a request to the bank's transfer endpoint, and the browser helpfully attaches the bank session cookie automatically, so the bank sees a valid authenticated request. The attacker never sees the password or cookie (eliminating A and D) — they ride the existing session.

questions.q: Distinguish cross-site scripting from cross-site request forgery using the episode's core hook.
options: XSS only works on banks; CSRF only works on social media sites
XSS forges a request that rides the victim's session; CSRF injects a script that runs in the browser
Both attacks steal the victim's password, but XSS is faster than CSRF
XSS injects a Script that runs in the victim's browser; CSRF injects no script but forges a request that rides the victim's already-authenticated session
questions.explanation: The episode's hook is 'XSS injects a Script; CSRF rides a Session.' XSS runs code as you by injecting script into the page; CSRF injects no code at all — it forges a request that abuses the trust the server already has in your authenticated browser. Option B is the exact reversal, which is the most common exam trap; getting the inject-script-versus-forge-request direction right is the mark.

questions.q: Explain how an anti-CSRF (synchroniser) token prevents cross-site request forgery.
options: It encrypts the session cookie so it cannot be read in transit

The server places a secret, unpredictable token in the legitimate form and validates it on every state-changing request; the attacker's page cannot read the token due to the same-origin policy, so the forged request is rejected
It logs the user out after each request so no session can be reused
It blocks all requests that do not come from a mobile device
questions.explanation: The server generates a secret, unpredictable token, embeds it in each legitimate form, and requires it back on every state-changing request, validating it server-side. Because the same-origin policy stops the attacker's cross-origin page from reading the token, a forged request cannot include the correct value and is rejected. This distinguishes genuine requests (which carry the token) from forged ones (which cannot) — naming the token, that the attacker can't read it, and server-side validation is the mark.

questions.q: Describe the open-redirect (invalid forwarding and redirecting) vulnerability and why it is dangerous.
options: The app caches old pages so users see outdated content; it is dangerous because data becomes stale
The app forwards all traffic through an attacker's proxy, decrypting it
The app redirects to a raw user-supplied URL, so an attacker crafts a link that begins at the trusted domain but sends the victim onward to a malicious phishing site
The app exposes its internal file paths in error messages, leaking source code
questions.explanation: An open redirect occurs when an app blindly redirects to whatever URL the request supplies. An attacker crafts a link that genuinely starts at your trusted domain — so the victim trusts and clicks it — but a redirect parameter sends them onward to a convincing fake login page that harvests credentials. It is gold for phishing because the link starts at the real domain, and the trap is treating it as harmless when it is a real phishing enabler.

questions.q: A developer protecting a money-transfer feature from CSRF says: 'I make sure the user is logged in before allowing the transfer.' Why does this answer earn no marks?
options: Because logged-in users are trusted and never need protection
Because being logged in is exactly the condition CSRF exploits — the forged request comes from an already-authenticated session, so requiring authentication does nothing to stop it
Because the developer should require the user to log out before each transfer
Because authentication only protects against XSS, not CSRF
questions.explanation: CSRF rides an already-authenticated session, so requiring the user to be logged in relies on the very thing the attack abuses — the forged request is sent precisely because the browser is authenticated. A strong answer instead names an anti-CSRF token (a secret the attacker cannot read or forge), explains why it is rejected, and reinforces with SameSite cookies set to Strict and re-authentication for high-value transfers. The token plus the why is the mark.

questions.q: A web application has a comment feature, a money-transfer feature, and a post-login redirect. Identify the vulnerability and the correct fix for each.
options: Comment: stored XSS, fix with output encoding and a Content Security Policy. Transfer: CSRF, fix with an anti-CSRF token plus SameSite cookies. Redirect: open redirect, fix by allow-listing destinations
Comment: CSRF, fix with allow-list. Transfer: open redirect, fix with output encoding. Redirect: stored XSS, fix with CSRF tokens
Comment: open redirect, fix with tokens. Transfer: stored XSS, fix with encoding. Redirect: CSRF, fix with SameSite cookies
All three features are vulnerable to SQL injection and are fixed by parameterised queries
questions.explanation: The comment feature is vulnerable to stored XSS (a saved script runs in every viewer's browser), fixed by output-encoding comments on display plus a Content Security Policy. The money-transfer feature is vulnerable to CSRF (a forged request from a logged-in browser), fixed by an anti-CSRF token plus SameSite cookies. The post-login redirect is vulnerable to an open redirect, fixed by validating the destination against an allow-list of internal locations — matching each feature to its specific vulnerability and targeted fix is full marks.
